one_line_takeaway: Attack success rate at a fixed query budget can hide order-of-magnitude differences in the compute an attacker must actually spend to jailbreak a model, and this compute-aware lens flips or complicates several standard safety-training conclusions -- most strikingly, RL-based safety alignment (SafeRL) can make a model strictly more exploitable per unit of adversarial compute under white-box GCG attacks even while it appears to help under other attacks and under raw ASR.
key_finding: Five main empirical findings on HarmBench (Table 1): (1) Non-monotonic effect of alignment stage on Tulu3-8B: SFT is the most robust stage (GCG and PAIR never reach C@0.5 within budget, i.e. C@0.5=infinity, vs. Base's C@0.5=59.3 TFLOPs for GCG), while further alignment via DPO (GCG C@0.5=521.2 TFLOPs, ASR@10=0.52) and especially RLVR (JailBroken ASR@10=0.90, a 40% increase over SFT's 0.50) erodes robustness in both ASR and compute terms. (2) Model scaling from Qwen2.5-Instruct 0.5B to 7B increases GCG's C@0.5 by 20x (20.0 to 399.7 TFLOPs) while ASR@10 falls from 0.99 to 0.73, but JailBroken's C@0.5 only grows 2.8x (8.2 to 22.8 TFLOPs) with ASR@10 barely dropping (0.99 to 0.94) -- scaling deters expensive gradient attacks far more than cheap template attacks. (3) GCG suffixes optimized on a surrogate (Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct) transfer to a separate closed proxy target (Qwen3-8B) achieving non-trivial ASR@10=0.15 (AE=4.9e-3) at a fraction of the cost, though risk plateaus rather than reaching the 50% threshold (C@0.5=infinity), indicating a ceiling set by suffix quality/target robustness rather than additional compute. (4) Compute cost to breach varies up to ~5x across harm categories within a single model (Qwen3-4B-SafeRL, comparing harassment & bullying vs. cybercrime & unauthorized intrusion under JailBroken). (5) Safety-RL (Qwen3-4B-SafeRL vs. base Qwen3-4B) raises aggregate cost under JailBroken (AE drops 22.1->16.0, C@0.5 rises 21.2->24.5 TFLOPs) and dramatically raises cost under GCG (base C@0.5=infinity vs SafeRL's finite 189.0 TFLOPs, counterintuitively -- SafeRL is strictly MORE exploitable than base under GCG at every compute level, attributed to a training-distribution mismatch between RL on natural-language adversarial prompts and GCG's logit-level token optimization) but backfires in specific categories (cybercrime, illegal activities, misinformation), where SafeRL's per-TFLOP exploitability (AE) exceeds the unaligned base model's.
core_contribution: The paper introduces a compute-aware evaluation framework for LLM jailbreak robustness that replaces fixed-query-budget Attack Success Rate (ASR) with computational pressure, i.e. cumulative FLOPs consumed by an attack. It formalizes 'risk-compute curves' (empirical risk as a function of average cumulative FLOPs per prompt) and derives two scalar summary metrics: C@tau (compute required for an attack to first reach tau=0.5 risk, with C@0.5=infinity if never reached) and Average Efficiency AE = CAURC / C_max (normalized expected risk gained per unit FLOP, via trapezoidal area under the risk-compute curve). The authors instantiate per-step FLOP cost models for three attack families (JailBroken template-based, PAIR iterative black-box refinement, GCG white-box gradient optimization) and run a systematic empirical study across 10 models (Tulu3-8B Base/SFT/DPO/RLVR; Qwen2.5-Instruct 0.5B/3B/7B; Qwen3-8B as a transfer target; Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-4B-SafeRL) on HarmBench and JailbreakBench, releasing the framework/code (r-three/risk-under-pressure) as open source.
threat_model: The paper models an adversary operating under a fixed query budget lambda (0 to 10 model queries) who runs an iterative refinement loop: propose a candidate prompt, query the target model M, evaluate the response with an automated safety-judge model (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct), and either stop on success or refine. Three attacker capability regimes are covered: white-box (GCG, requiring gradient access to target weights), black-box iterative (PAIR, uses an auxiliary attacker LLM, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, to rewrite prompts based on the target's prior responses), and black-box template-based (JailBroken, applies fixed obfuscation templates such as prefix injection, role-play/AIM persona, Base64 encoding). A separate attack-transfer threat model is examined: an attacker without weight access to a closed target instead optimizes GCG suffixes on an open-weight surrogate (Tulu3-8B-DPO or Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct in the transfer experiment) and transfers the resulting suffix to a different closed/proxy target (Qwen3-8B), reflecting realistic black-box exploitation of white-box attacks. The core framing explicitly imports the classical security 'work factor' concept (adversary effort to breach a system under realistic budgets, citing Kerckhoffs/Schneier/Menezes) into LLM jailbreak evaluation, arguing defenders should aim to raise adversarial compute cost rather than pursue zero ASR.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 7): (1) Standard transformer FLOPs is a theoretical proxy for attacker effort and may not reflect real-world constraints such as memory bandwidth, latency, and parallelizability. (2) Results depend on automated LLM-judge reliability, which the authors note can have false positives/negatives and category bias, citing Schwinn et al. 2026 and Ye et al. 2025 on judge unreliability, plus possible output-truncation artifacts from computational resource constraints. (3) Only three attack families were evaluated (GCG, PAIR, JailBroken) without adaptive attacks that jointly optimize against a specific defense, which the authors say would likely tighten robustness bounds further. (4) The binary success metric treats all jailbreaks as equally severe, ignoring harm severity/utility gain to the attacker. (5) The study is limited to models up to 8B parameters and did not extend to larger scales, higher query budgets, closed-weight frontier models, or multimodal settings, which the authors flag as open directions.

quantitative_results: Tulu3-8B Base: GCG C@0.5=59.3 TFLOPs, ASR@10=1.00 (all attacks)
Tulu3-8B SFT: GCG C@0.5=infinity, PAIR C@0.5=infinity, JailBroken C@0.5=52.4 TFLOPs, ASR@10 = 0.31 (GCG) / 0.42 (PAIR) / 0.50 (JailBroken)
Tulu3-8B DPO: GCG C@0.5=521.2 TFLOPs, PAIR C@0.5=79.9 TFLOPs, ASR@10=0.52 (GCG)
Tulu3-8B RLVR: JailBroken ASR@10=0.90 (40% above SFT, 23% above DPO); JailBroken AE=18.9e-3 (1.8x DPO's, 2.1x SFT's)

Qwen2.5-Instruct scaling (0.5B -> 7B): GCG C@0.5 20.0 -> 399.7 TFLOPs (20x increase over 15x size increase), ASR@10 0.99 -> 0.73; JailBroken C@0.5 8.2 -> 22.8 TFLOPs (2.8x), ASR@10 0.99 -> 0.94; GCG AE reduced 19.7x across the size range vs. JailBroken AE reduced only 2.6x
Attack transfer: GCG suffixes from Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct to Qwen3-8B: ASR@10=0.15, AE=4.9e-3, C@0.5=infinity
Qwen3-4B vs Qwen3-4B-SafeRL under JailBroken: ASR@10 0.83 (SafeRL) vs 0.86 (base); AE 16.0 (SafeRL) vs 22.1 (base); C@0.5 24.5 (SafeRL) vs 21.2 TFLOPs (base)
Qwen3-4B vs Qwen3-4B-SafeRL under GCG: base C@0.5=infinity, SafeRL C@0.5=189.0 TFLOPs; AE 0.9 (base) -> 2.1e-3 (SafeRL), more than doubling exploitability
Compute cost to reach 50% risk varies ~5x across harm categories for Qwen3-4B-SafeRL under JailBroken
Cross-benchmark consistency between HarmBench and JailbreakBench: Spearman rho >= 0.91 across all metrics
GCG step cost sensitivity: varying beta_bwd in {2,3,4} changes total GCG step cost by ±1% for large models and ±5% for 0.5B models
baselines_compared: JailBroken (Wei et al. 2023) - template-based attack with 8 obfuscation strategies
PAIR (Chao et al. 2025) - black-box iterative refinement attack using Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct as attacker
GCG / Greedy Coordinate Gradient (Zou et al. 2023) - white-box gradient-based suffix optimization attack
Standard fixed-query-budget ASR as the conventional evaluation baseline being critiqued throughout
